content.text: OSS license compliance scanning is the automated process of identifying all software components and their associated licenses in a GenAI codebase, classifying them against the organization's license policy, and generating actionable findings in the development workflow. For GenAI applications, scanning must extend beyond traditional Python and JavaScript dependency trees to include container images, AI model weights, and training datasets, each of which carries its own licensing obligations.

Scanning is the technical enforcement layer that gives the OSS usage policy operational teeth. Without automated scanning, license compliance depends entirely on developers manually checking each dependency they add — a process that fails under time pressure and that cannot catch license changes introduced by upgrading existing dependencies. Scanning integrated into CI/CD catches violations at the pull request level, before they enter the main branch or reach production.

Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generation and OSS license compliance scanning are moving from best practice to regulatory and customer requirement. The US Executive Order on Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity (14028) requires SBOM for software sold to the federal government. The EU Cyber Resilience Act, which will apply to connected digital products including AI-enabled products sold in the EU, includes provisions for component transparency consistent with SBOM generation. Large enterprise customers increasingly require SBOM artifacts as part of procurement security questionnaires.

For GenAI specifically, the risks of unscanned license compliance are higher than for traditional software because AI model weights and datasets are routinely shared on Hugging Face and similar repositories without comprehensive SPDX-format license metadata. A model published with an ambiguous or undisclosed license creates legal risk that a scanning tool configured only for standard SPDX licenses will miss.
Catches copyleft license violations at pull request time before they enter the main branch
Generates SBOM artifacts required for regulatory compliance and enterprise customer procurement requirements
Identifies license changes when dependencies are upgraded, which otherwise go unnoticed until a legal audit
Provides a continuous inventory of all OSS components for M&A due diligence and insurance requirements
Reduces legal review burden by automating the classification of components with known licenses, directing Legal's attention to ambiguous cases
Creates an audit trail of license compliance decisions that supports the organization's defense in intellectual property disputes
Broad scanning that catches everything generates noise for components with unusual but benign license expressions; tuning the scanner requires investment
Blocking the CI on review-required licenses creates friction for legitimate Tier 2 components; warning and requiring documented review is a common compromise
SBOM completeness vs. AI artifact coverage:
Standard SBOM tools cover code well but have limited support for AI model and dataset artifacts; AI-specific SBOM fields (CycloneDX ML) are still maturing
Scanning only on PRs misses license changes introduced by upstream package updates between PRs; daily or weekly scheduled scans of the main branch catch these

The scanning pipeline operates at multiple levels: code dependencies, container images, and AI artifacts. Each level uses different tools and produces inputs to the central policy engine and SBOM generator.

Code dependency scanning analyzes the package manifest files (requirements.txt, pyproject.toml for Python; package.json for Node.js; Cargo.toml for Rust; go.mod for Go) and resolves the complete dependency graph including transitive dependencies. The scanner identifies the license for each package by consulting SPDX license databases, package registry metadata, and embedded license files.

For Python GenAI applications, common high-risk transitive dependencies include Hugging Face Transformers (Apache 2.0 — safe), PyTorch (BSD — safe), and various research code libraries that use AGPL or GPL licenses. The scanner must traverse at least three to four levels of transitive dependencies to reliably catch copyleft components.

Container images used in GenAI serving infrastructure include OS packages from the base image (Ubuntu, Debian, Alpine) in addition to Python packages. OS packages have their own license metadata, typically in /usr/share/doc, and include GPL-licensed components that are part of the OS kernel and system tools but are typically not distributed in a way that triggers copyleft obligations. Scanner tools that understand Linux OS package licenses correctly distinguish these from application-level GPL inclusions.

AI model scanning is the least mature part of the pipeline. Hugging Face model repository cards include a license field, but many popular models use custom licenses (Meta Llama Community License, Mistral's license) that do not appear in the SPDX license identifier database. The scanning workflow for AI models typically involves: querying the model card license field, looking up the license text in the organization's AI model license register, and flagging models with unknown or non-standard licenses for manual review.

Training dataset scanning follows the same pattern: check the dataset card license field on Hugging Face or Kaggle, map to the organization's approved/review/prohibited tiers, and flag datasets with data-specific licenses (CC BY-SA, database rights) for Legal review before use in training.

The policy engine maps scanner output to the organization's three-tier classification and generates findings. Prohibited licenses block the CI build and require the engineer to either replace the component or initiate the formal exception process. Review-required licenses generate a warning and open a ticket in the review queue; the CI build can proceed but the ticket must be resolved before release. Approved licenses pass silently and are added to the SBOM.

The SBOM is generated from the scanner output on every tagged release. SPDX and CycloneDX are the two primary SBOM format standards; CycloneDX has AI-specific extensions (CycloneDX ML) that can represent model weights, datasets, and training environment metadata. The SBOM is stored in the artifact registry alongside the release and made available to customers and regulators on request.

attrs.alt: OSS License Compliance Scanning Pipeline for GenAI

content.text: The diagram shows the five-stage scanning pipeline from source code and AI artifacts through scanner, policy engine, triage, and SBOM output. The lower section shows the two main axes of the pipeline: what to scan (code dependencies, container images, and AI artifacts) and the remediation workflow for prohibited, review-required, and approved findings.
On every pull request that introduces a new dependency, as a blocking or warning CI check
On a scheduled basis (weekly or monthly) to detect license changes introduced by upstream package updates between PRs
On every tagged release to generate the SBOM artifact
When adding a new AI model or training dataset to the codebase or model registry
Before any M&A due diligence, customer security questionnaire, or regulatory submission that requires SBOM or OSS compliance evidence
Step 1: Choose and configure a scanning tool

Select a scanning tool appropriate for your technology stack. FOSSA supports all major package managers and has CI/CD integrations for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins. Black Duck provides enterprise-grade scanning with a large license database. For lighter-weight needs, pip-licenses (Python), license-checker (Node.js), and go-licenses (Go) are open-source alternatives suitable for pre-commit hooks.

Configure the scanner with the organization's three-tier license policy. Most enterprise tools support policy-as-code where the approved, review-required, and prohibited license lists are defined in a configuration file versioned alongside the codebase. Define separate policies for code dependencies, container images, and AI artifacts if the classifications differ.

Add the scanner as a CI/CD step. For code dependencies, the scan runs on every PR. The CI step is configured to fail (block the build) on prohibited licenses and to warn (pass the build but generate an alert) on review-required licenses. Configure the scanner to generate a license report artifact that is uploaded to the CI run for review.
Step 4: Set up the SBOM generation step

Add an SBOM generation step to the release pipeline triggered by a tagged release. Configure the output format as CycloneDX JSON with AI-specific fields populated for model and dataset components. Store the SBOM in the artifact registry with the same version tag as the release.

Define where findings go for human review. Review-required findings should create tickets in the engineering project management system, assigned to a license review queue monitored by Legal or the compliance team. Prohibited findings should block the PR with a message directing the engineer to replace the component or initiate the exception process.

# GitHub Actions workflow for OSS license scanning
name: OSS License Compliance
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, develop]
  push:
    tags: ['v*']

jobs:
  license-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt pip-licenses

      - name: Run license scan
        run: |
          pip-licenses --format=json --with-license-file \
            --output-file license-report.json

      - name: Check for prohibited licenses
        run: |
          python3 - << 'PYEOF'
          import json, sys
          PROHIBITED = {"GPL-2.0", "GPL-3.0", "AGPL-3.0", "SSPL-1.0"}
          REVIEW_REQUIRED = {"LGPL-2.1", "LGPL-3.0", "MPL-2.0", "EPL-2.0"}
          
          with open("license-report.json") as f:
              packages = json.load(f)
          
          blocked = []
          flagged = []
          for pkg in packages:
              lic = pkg.get("License", "")
              if any(p in lic for p in PROHIBITED):
                  blocked.append(f"{pkg['Name']} ({lic})")
              elif any(r in lic for r in REVIEW_REQUIRED):
                  flagged.append(f"{pkg['Name']} ({lic})")
          
          if flagged:
              print("::warning::Review-required licenses found:")
              for f in flagged:
                  print(f"::warning::  REVIEW: {f}")
          
          if blocked:
              print("::error::PROHIBITED licenses found:")
              for b in blocked:
                  print(f"::error::  BLOCKED: {b}")
              sys.exit(1)
          
          print("License check passed.")
          PYEOF

      - name: Upload license report
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
        with:
          name: license-report
          path: license-report.json

  # SBOM generation on release tags
  sbom-generate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install CycloneDX
        run: pip install cyclonedx-bom

      - name: Generate SBOM
        run: |
          cyclonedx-py requirements \
            --format json \
            --output sbom-${{ github.ref_name }}.cdx.json

      - name: Upload SBOM to release
        uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v1
        with:
          files: sbom-${{ github.ref_name }}.cdx.json

A GenAI startup built an agent framework using LangChain as the primary orchestration library. When their FOSSA license scan ran for the first time as part of a SOC 2 Type II audit preparation, it identified that a transitive dependency of an older version of LangChain pulled in an AGPL-3.0-licensed text processing library. Because the startup offered its framework as a SaaS API, AGPL's network-use copyleft clause potentially required them to open-source their entire application.

The FOSSA report provided exact dependency paths showing where the AGPL library entered the tree. The engineering team upgraded LangChain to a newer version that had replaced the AGPL dependency with an MIT-licensed alternative, resolving the issue. The incident prompted the team to add FOSSA scanning to their CI/CD pipeline and enable daily scheduled scans of the main branch to catch similar issues introduced by upstream upgrades.
Prohibited licenses are almost always in transitive dependencies, not direct imports; full graph resolution is mandatory

OS packages in container images have licenses; while most are GPL-licensed at the OS level in ways that do not trigger copyleft obligations for the application, some distributions include problematic packages
Ignoring AI model and dataset licenses in the scanner:
Standard scanners do not cover model weights or datasets; these require a separate lookup process or custom scanner integration
Upstream packages update their licenses; a daily or weekly scan of the main branch catches license changes introduced without a new PR
An SBOM that is generated once and not regenerated on each release quickly becomes stale and misleading in due diligence or regulatory contexts
Scan the full transitive dependency graph, not only direct dependencies
Integrate scanning into CI/CD as a blocking gate for prohibited licenses and a warning gate for review-required licenses
Run scheduled daily or weekly scans of the main branch to catch license changes introduced by upstream package updates
Maintain a separate AI model license registry for models and datasets not covered by standard SPDX scanning tools
Generate a new SBOM on every tagged release and store it in the artifact registry alongside the release
Include container image scanning alongside code dependency scanning; OS packages have licenses too
Review scanner findings with Legal quarterly to tune the policy configuration and resolve accumulated review-required tickets

At scale, license scanning can add meaningful time to CI/CD builds. Commercial tools typically take 30 seconds to 3 minutes for most codebases. For large monorepos or codebases with hundreds of services, consider caching the dependency graph between builds and only re-scanning changed services. Scheduled full scans of the main branch separate from PR scanning allow thorough analysis without blocking development velocity.

SBOM generation for large multi-service GenAI platforms produces multiple per-service SBOMs that must be aggregated into a product-level SBOM for customer delivery. Invest in SBOM tooling that supports aggregation and merging, and define which SBOM scope corresponds to each customer-facing product.
Commercial license compliance platform with policy-as-code, CI/CD integration, SBOM generation in SPDX and CycloneDX, and a large license database; strong on AI-augmented license detection
Enterprise-grade OSS scanning with vulnerability and license detection; known for comprehensive transitive dependency support
Developer-friendly scanner with IDE integration, GitHub integration, and license policy enforcement; good for shift-left scanning
Open-source SBOM generator supporting SPDX and CycloneDX; integrates with container image scanning
Vulnerability scanner that works with syft SBOMs; useful for combining license and vulnerability scanning in one pipeline
CycloneDX BOM format extensions for AI/ML artifacts including model metadata, training data provenance, and model card fields

The OSS scanning tool itself is part of the security supply chain. A scanner that produces false negatives (missing prohibited licenses) provides a false sense of compliance. Validate the scanner periodically by injecting a known-prohibited license into a test branch and confirming the scanner catches it. Also validate the SBOM output against a manually curated subset of the dependency tree to confirm accuracy.

Scanner results are sensitive IP: the component manifest reveals your technology stack in detail. Store scan results and SBOMs with access controls appropriate for confidential business information, and consider what SBOM fields to share publicly versus providing only to specific customers under NDA.
Scan the full transitive dependency graph; prohibited licenses are almost always in transitive, not direct, dependencies
Extend scanning beyond code to container images, AI model weights, and training datasets — each has licensing obligations
Integrate scanning into CI/CD as a blocking gate for prohibited licenses so violations are caught at the PR level, not post-deployment
Run scheduled daily or weekly scans of the main branch to catch license changes introduced by upstream package updates between PRs
Generate a new SBOM on every tagged release in CycloneDX or SPDX format and store it in the artifact registry
Maintain a separate AI model license registry for AI-specific licenses not covered by standard SPDX scanning tools
Validate scanner accuracy periodically by injecting known-prohibited licenses into test branches to confirm detection
